coredata.dc:title: Green carbon dots with multifaceted applications– Waste to wealth strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Salimi Shahraki, Hesam
Ahmad, Anees
Bushra, Rani
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel member of the carbon family, are recently emerging as a potential substitute for traditional fluorescent materials. Compared to chemically derived CDs, green CDs produced from the renewable resources drawn much attention owing to their distinctive features such as cost effective, non-toxic nature, tunable luminescence, improved aqueous solubility and high biocompatibility. Using sustainable raw material and biomass for the development of functional material promotes the minimization of waste production. In this review, we outline and briefly discuss various synthesis routes, fluorescent mechanisms and precursors required to form the CDs, with a primary emphasis on the eco-friendly hydrothermal approach. Different heteroatoms in the form of functional groups available on the surface of green CDs help improve their quantum yield, physicochemical, and optical properties without surface passivation. Moreover, CDs tunable and up- photoluminescence behavior (PL) led to their remarkable usage in sensing, drug delivery, and bioimaging. Further, the importance of food wastes (animal, plant, and food processing byproducts) as the source of carbon which is often overlooked, is also reported herein. The promising applications of CDs in bioimaging, fluorescent inks, drug delivery, catalysis, and heavy metal ions detection were also elaborated. In the end, an outlook on the opportunities, possible challenges, and appealing prospects concerning the beneficial exploitation of renewable resources are discussed.

head.ce:title: Green carbon dots with multifaceted applications– Waste to wealth strategy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel member of the carbon family, are recently emerging as a potential substitute for traditional fluorescent materials. Compared to chemically derived CDs, green CDs produced from the renewable resources drawn much attention owing to their distinctive features such as cost effective, non-toxic nature, tunable luminescence, improved aqueous solubility and high biocompatibility. Using sustainable raw material and biomass for the development of functional material promotes the minimization of waste production. In this review, we outline and briefly discuss various synthesis routes, fluorescent mechanisms and precursors required to form the CDs, with a primary emphasis on the eco-friendly hydrothermal approach. Different heteroatoms in the form of functional groups available on the surface of green CDs help improve their quantum yield, physicochemical, and optical properties without surface passivation. Moreover, CDs tunable and up- photoluminescence behavior (PL) led to their remarkable usage in sensing, drug delivery, and bioimaging. Further, the importance of food wastes (animal, plant, and food processing byproducts) as the source of carbon which is often overlooked, is also reported herein. The promising applications of CDs in bioimaging, fluorescent inks, drug delivery, catalysis, and heavy metal ions detection were also elaborated. In the end, an outlook on the opportunities, possible challenges, and appealing prospects concerning the beneficial exploitation of renewable resources are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are a kind of fluorescent carbon material with a diameter below 10 nm. It is mainly composed of an amorphous carbon matrix with an embedded sp2 conjugated nanocrystalline core. CDs are interesting because of their exceptional properties and applications. The most salient feature of CDs is their fluorescence which made them available for bioimaging [1,2], biosensing [3,4], catalysis [5,6], nanomedicine [7,8], chemical sensor [9-11], energy research [12,13], and gene delivery applications[14,15]. Moreover, CDs chemical, physical and optical properties can be ameliorated through surface passivationby various chemical groups. For the preparation of these key materials, synthetic approaches can be categorized into two: top-down and bottom-up methods [16]. The macroscopic carbon sources are used for producing the CDs from a top-down approach. At the same time small molecules with functional groups (–OH, –COOH, -C = O, and –NH2) are utilized as a precursor of carbon in the bottom-up approach[17]. The former methods include electrochemical oxidation[18], arc discharge[19], and laser ablation[20], which involves harsh experimental conditions, limited spectral efficiency, not enough product yield, shortage of size control, tedious operation steps, and expensive equipment, which limit their practical applications. As a result, researchers are more likely to apply the bottom-up strategy because it utilizes natural materials without toxic chemicals. Moreover, this approach helps produce nanostructures with homogeneous chemical composition and fewer defects and better short/long-range orders. Hydrothermal treatment is an efficient and convenient methodamong the numerous protocols considering the ease of manipulating reaction conditions, facile instrumentation technique, and high atom economy. Moreover, it does not require any post-synthetic surface passivation [21].

In the past few years, significant progress has been made in producing CDs with distinct properties and applications, as reviewed by Hanzhi et al.[22], Himaja et al.[23], Bruno et al.[24], Trong et al.[25], and Chao Kang et al.[26].CDs displayed excitation and emission characteristics at different wavelengths depending on their size, considering them as one of the most critical parameters. The controllable synthesis of CDs is currently still in its infancy.

The CDs produced from the green sources are termed as “green CDs”. The green resources relate to the materials that are biomass or renewable natural products, include plants, vegetables, fruits, beverages, juices, bakery products, and human derivatives. Green precursors are an excellent source for the synthesis of CDs because they are sustainable, eco-friendly, pronounced yield, inexpensive, easy to obtain, high renewability, and carbon source abundance. Researchers primarily focus on the nature and application of CDs, ignoring the significance of carbon sources. The production of CDs from natural resources is the valuable transformation of low-value waste into value-added products. Different type of wastes that includes seeds [27], grains[28], leaves[29], fruits[30] ,coffee[31],milk[32] and tea[33] had been considered as a sustainable green precursor to produce CDs. Nevertheless, it is also extracted from available food sources such as caramel[34] and Nescafe[35]. Biomass synthesized CDs have great potential for biomedical applications in energy storage systems, environmental research, heavy metal ions detection, food and water purification processes[36]. Xu et al. [37], Yang et al. [38], and Wei et al. [39] synthesized CDs from green tea, honey, and cornflour employed for metal ions detection. Nowadays, scientists also focused their attention on the utilization of food waste (good source of carbon) as a starting precursor in CDs production to avoid the wastage of the food available for human consumption which costs about 1.3 billion tons/yr [40]. CDs produced from green tea ,and ginger juice successfully detect the Hg2+ ions and are helpful in inhibiting the growth of liver tumors in a mouse models without affecting the mammary epithelial cells[41,42]. The temperature needed for the preparation of CDs through the green route is comparatively smaller than chemically-derived CDs. Besides, the production of CDs is also dependent on the type of solvent needed for dissolving the starting resources. Natural precursors don’t require toxic chemicals for their dissolution. In addition, quantum yield (QY) is heavily reliant on the source of carbon and the method used.

Due to their broad spectrum of applications, there is the possibility of CDs in the ecosystem, which poses serious health hazards to the living creatures. Havrdova et al.[43] determined the influence of charged CDs on mouse fibroblasts and suggested that neutrally charged CDs were less toxic than the positively charged, which instigated the changes in the cell cycle (G0 phase). Therefore, in terms of assessing the safety of CDs, more nano-toxicity testing work is needed when applying in the food industry. Thus, CDs are a growing star of carbon-nanomaterials owing to their fabulous physicochemical, electrical, and optical properties.

This review addresses the recent progress on the characteristics of CDs fabricated from green sources and their emerging applications to meet the interest of relevant researchers in this field. Concerning CDs applications, we particularly emphasize bioimaging, sensing, catalysis, and drug delivery. Furthermore, this review will also help the researchers gain a better knowledge of the importance of green CDs as a fluorescent nano-sensor for detecting metal ions to remediate the contamination of pollutants and associated problems. The utilization of natural resources is the best green pathway employed by most researchers for synthesizing the CDs as it is cheap, renewable, abundant biomass, helped in the waste management process, and reduced chemical exposure. Lastly, the challenges and future directions to realize the full potential of CDs in environmental systems are also highlighted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food waste as a carbon source
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, food waste which is considered a good source of carbon is undervalued. To utilize its economic and nutritional value, it can be transformed to value-added products. The main disposal methods for food waste include landing and burning, and the potential to use them is a significant apprehension. The proper treatment of food waste is the only way that provides a platform for many inventions in agriculture, biomedicine, and industry. Their transformation into functional material is the only pathway to manage environmental pollution. Food wastes such as orange juice[44], lemon peel[45], orange peel[46], pollen and sugar cane molasses[47], etc., have a sufficient amount of carbon content necessary to act as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs. The use of food waste as a carbon source has piqued the scientific community's curiosity due to its remarkable properties and applications that assisted the economic and environmental sectors[48]. Hence, developing a simple, inexpensive, and eco-friendly method is very crucial for waste management. Therefore, the hydrothermal method is preferred among other methods for synthesizing green CDs because it is simple, convenient, and eco-friendly. The obtained CDs possessed favorable good QY and optical properties. In the literature, researchers combined the two methods for fabricating CDs; pyrolysis–carbonization [44], and hydrothermal-carbonization[49,50].

Peels of fruits and vegetables are rich in amino, sulfur, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups depend on the synthesis approach. These functional groups act as excellent chelating ligands that improve the optical and physicochemical properties with a broad spectrum of applications. The benefits of synthesizing CDs from food waste include easy access to the carbon source, easy preparation, and the possibility of large-scale production. Food waste is a possible carbon source for the synthesis of CDs in the food industry. Beer, bread, hamburger, honey, pizza, and caramels are also employed to produce CDs that reminds us of the presence of the nanostructures in food items[51]. Food-borne CDs are successfully applied in biomedical applications. Therefore, CDs produced from food waste could have a brighter future in managing food quality and safety detection [52].

Plant by-products are a valuable carbon source in the food industry for producing CDs because of their excellent biocompatibility, reasonable, and benign nature. Alam et al. hydrothermally synthesized CDs at 140 °C for 5 hby utilizing cabbage as the carbon source[53]. The resultant CDs have shown down and up-conversion PL properties. The presence of nitrogen and oxygen on CDs surface contributes to their high luminescence by inducing an upward shift in the Fermi level and increased QY. Moreover, oxygen and nitrogen groups help enhance the physicochemical and optical properties of CDs. The produced CDs showed considerable promise in biomedical application.

Many articles reported that the by-products of vegetables have the highest quantum yield values compared to animal and industrial waste used for producing CDs. Raji et al[54] utilized banana peel waste for synthesizing the fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal method. UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy determined the optical properties. The synthesized CDs produced intense blue fluorescence under the excitation of UV-light (365 nm) with a good QY of 20%. It is successfully applied for cell imaging applications. The UV–vis absorption spectra of synthesized CDs (Fig. 1 (a) showed the absorption band at 277 and 322 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* and n- π* transitions of C = C and C = O hybrid orbitals. From the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 1(b&c) at different wavelengths, CDs showed the strongest fluorescence at 355 nm, after that, there is a gradual decrease in the intensities. With the increasing wavelength from 290 to 460 nm, a redshift is observed in the spectra, as shown in Fig. 1(d). Fluorescence photostability is one of the most significant parameters for biological application. Hence, the synthesized CDs showed excellent photostability for a longer time demonstrating him to function as an excellent probe in cellular imaging. Highly photoluminescent (PL) CDs from orange juice are prepared hydrothermally with QY noted to be 26%, comparably higher than carbon nanomaterial synthesized by other methods. As evident from FTIR and XPS analysis, the presence of functional groups (C = O, C-OH, C-O-C and C-H) on the surface of CDs are responsible for their excellent solubility in aqueous media. Moreover, they do not show photobleaching behavior. They have low size distribution (1.5–4.5 nm) and can be exploited in biomedical applications due to their excellent photostability and low cyto-toxicity[44].

A facile and cost-effective hydrothermal approach was employed to synthesize CDs produced from papaya as a natural carbon precursor, using water and ethanol as solvent at 200 °C for 5 h. Due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, CDs can act as a probe for sensing the Fe3+ ions with a limit of detection of 0.48 μmolL−1 and 0.29 μmolL−1, respectively [55]. CDs are synthesized from lemon peel hydrothermally in Teflon lined autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h. Hydrothermal carbonization imparts surface functionalization, which makes CDs water-soluble and photoluminescent. Conversion of organic material to CDs involved many steps which include dehydration, fragmentation, condensation, aromatization, and carbonization. Under UV light irradiation, the aqueous solution of CDs showed bright blue luminescence. Zeta potential values (-57.9 mV) demonstrated the existence of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of CDs. The tunable emissive nature of CDs is determined by fluorescence emission spectra. Up to now, the PL mechanism for tunable emissive CDs is not clearly understood. Many researchers assumed it was due to the quantum confinement effect or particles' surface molecular states [56,57]. Papaya derived CDs worked as a highly sensitive fluorescent probes to selectively sense the Cr6+ ions with response time determined to be 1 min ,and detection limit noted to be 73 nM [58]. Mangosteen, pineapple, and grapefruit peels are used for synthesizing the CDs [59-61]. The prepared CDs from the watermelon peel had excellent PL properties and are quite stable under varying pH conditions and high salt concentrations[62].

Tea-based fluorescent nano-sensor produced from the waste of tea extract exhibited fluorescence (bright blue) on irradiation with UV–vis light at 365 nm. Tea CDs represented exceptional stability and fluorescence intensity. As an on–off sensor, it can quantitatively characterize CrO4 2−, Fe3+ ions, ascorbic acid, and L-Cys. It is more economical, highly sensitive, and has potential applications in the environmental and analytical chemistry fields. The QY was determined to be 7.1% [63]. Multi emissive CDs hybrids were derived from bamboo leaves via hydrothermal treatment by mixing the dry leaves with 120 mL ethanol and heated at 100 °C for 24 h. Later, it was filtered, and the filtrate was mixed with 5 mL of water and kept in an autoclave at 80 °C for 5 h. The porphyrin derivatives are available on the CDs surface show varyingPLproperties. These groups interacted with the Pb2+ ions with quenching and enhanced fluorescence signal observed at 493 and 653 nm, respectively[64]. These CDs sensed Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions in real water samples. Similarly, several more fruits and vegetable wastes such as Mango peel[65], Peel of pomegranate[66], Onion peel [67], Allium sativum peel [68], Sweet potato peel [69], Waste acorn cups[70], Olive waste[71], Coconut coir[72], Aloe peel [73], Date kernel [74], Durian peel[75], Coconut waste[76], Passion fruit shells[77], Lemon peel [78], and Onion juice[79] are also exploited for producing green CDs (Table 1 ). The intracellular fluorescence imaging using the peel of Allium sativum is shown in Fig. 2 , which certifies the promising prospectsof the CDs obtained from the kitchen waste for biological applications.

Cereals are the important part of our daily diet; it is a nutritionally rich source of dietary protein, carbohydrate, fiber, minerals, and many vitamins. Several types of cereals are grown worldwide, with some structural similarities. The processing of cereals generates a large amount of by-products that are utilized in the production of CDs. One-third of cereals and almost half of all fruits and vegetables are wasted across the world. Rice residue and glycine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources for hydrothermally generating nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs). The N-CDs showed fluorescence (420–500 nm) with maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm as observed from fluorescence emission and excitation spectra. The maximum intensity of the emission peak shifted to a longer wavelength due to the difference in the particle size of CDs and emissive trap sites distribution. The average size was determined to be 2.70 nm. N-CDs obtained from the rice residue, and glycine has excellent stability, high solubility, and improved fluorescence intensity. It act as an efficient sensor for Fe3+ and tetracycline (TCs), terramycin, andchlortetracycline with a detection limit of 0.7462 , 0.2367, 0.3739, and 0.2791 µM respectively [80]. It also showed good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and high permeability. Khan et al. prepared water-soluble N- CDs by red lentils via the hydrothermal method to detect Fe3+ ions. Effect of ionic concentration and pH studies established that the sensor is stable in high salt conditions, and has no effect of pH. FTIR and XPS studies confirmed the presence of amide and hydroxyl groups on the surface. Similar to other CDs, these also show the excitation- dependent emission characteristics[81]. Xiahong et al. reported the preparation of CDs (average particle size: 3.3 nm) with unique excitation-dependent behavior, excellent optical characteristics, and high photostability from the peanut shells. Fluorescence-quenching trials were carried out to determine the sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ ions. The detection limit was determined to be 4.8 μM. Moreover, the aqueous solution of CDs emits blue-green light, under UV irradiation, having a QY of 10.58% [82]. CDs produced hydrothermally from cranberry beans do not require hazardous chemicals, surface passivation, and complex instrumentation. In other words, the synthesis process is environmentally friendly. Fluorescence intensity quenching of CDs induced by the addition of Fe3+ ions was much higher compared to other heavy metal ions as reported. The resultant CDs can detect the Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 30–600 μM with a detection limit of 9.55 μM [83]. Vinoth Kumar et al. [84] prepared fluorescent CDs using rice bran without any surface passivating agent. Upon irradiation with UV-light, CDs produced green emission; QY and particle size was noted to be 7.4% and 2.96 nm, respectively. Rice bran-derived CDs effectively remove the dye from industrial wastewater with a degradation efficiency of 89.20% in 30 min. Wang et al.[85] fabricated CDs from rice biomass by hydrothermal method. The synthesized CDs exhibited bright and tunable PL with excellent biocompatibility. Corn bract-derived CDs prepared solvothermally is successfully exploited as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ ions. It exhibited the dual fluorescence emission behavior due to the intrinsic structure of CDs and chlorophyll-derived porphyrins, respectively. This nanohybrid sensor has high sensitivity and satisfying detection performance in serum and river water samples[86]. The fluorescence spectra of synthesized CDs from sago waste via the pyrolysis method specified the quenching of emission peaks after the addition of different metal ions. It exhibited the potential to be worked as an optical probe for sensing metal ions in aqueous media[87]. Similarly, several more kinds of cereal such as wheat straw[88], chickpea peel [89], wheat straw & bamboo residue [90], rice residue [80], fenugreek seed [91], and sorghum, pearl millet, rice[92], are also exploited for producing green CDs (Table 1).

Coffee beans are a rich source of carbohydrates, proteins, antioxidants, and amino acids, together with other nutritional ingredients. Akhiruddin et al. [93] prepared CDs by utilizing coffee grounds (CG) as a carbon source. CG (2.5 g) was added to 0.2 M NaOH (250 mL) solution with continuous stirring until adequately mixed and finally autoclaved at 100° C for 1 h. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) determined the size of CDs to be 7.74 nm. The hydrothermally synthesized CDs prepared from wasted CG show uniform morphology, hydrophilic nature, and graphitic structure [94]. Liang and his workers explore coffee driven CDs as a fluorescent probe for imaging HeLa cells. After injecting the probe solution around the nucleus, the cell cytoplasm showed enhanced fluorescence, which clearly indicates that CDs entered the cell and remained emissive. It also worked as a nanosensor for the detection of 16 metal ions. Among them, only Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions successfully quench the PL of the CDs. The strong binding capacity and fast chelating kinetics of metal ions with the functional groups of CDs are responsible for the PL quenching. Zhang et al.[95] produced CDs from coffee bean shells by a mild molecular aggregation method. The coffee bean shells solution prepared using 4% NaOH was acidified (pH 2) and kept overnight and later washed, dried, and dialyzed. Coffee-borne CDs have strong anti-oxidation properties with high biocompatibility and application in bio-imaging. Many studies reported the production of CDs hydrothermally(150 °C, 5 h) from discarded tea waste extract as a carbon precursor [96]. The synthesized CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence on irradiation with UV-light and successfully detected the CrO4 2−, Fe3+, AA, and L-Cys in real water samples. It was effective in removing radicals in vitro (hydroxyland superoxide anion). Green tea leaf residue was preferred over tea leaf as the carbon source when viewed from conservation of resources. Fluorescent CDs prepared from residue via the bottom up method were highly stable towards pH, ionic strength, and UV radiation, but they are highly sensitive to temperature conditions. Tea CDs were successfully applied for the detection of gefitinib in urine samples [97]. Similarly, oolong tea and black tea employed to prepeare CDs hydrothermally showed excellent biocompatibility, high photostability, less cytotoxicity, and high sensitivity[98,99].

Fish by-products, dairy by-products, animal feathers, etc are considered in animal waste by-products. Yongming et al. [100] employed hair waste as a carbon source for synthesizing highly fluorescent CDs by thermal treatment. It exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior and excellent photostability, and good sensing ability to detect Hg2+ ions. Microwave-assisted CDs produced from the waste of animal skin were prepared by heating the pre-roasted skin at 750 W for 25 min. Later, ultrasonication and filtration led to CDs formation, and harnessed to mark Escherichia coli[101]. Seafood wastes that are either discarded in the sea or landfill are a challenging environmental issue. The best way is to transform them into valuable materials. One of the studies reported the utilization of industrial waste crab shells for producing CDs with excellent PL properties via the sonochemical method. The XPS studies determined the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups responsible for the aqueous solubility of CDs and their functionalization. Oxygen and nitrogen groups on the surface of CDs are accountable for their efficient PL behaviour. In vitro cytotoxicity studies showed excellent biocompatible behavior of CDs towards HeLa cells. The confocal microscopy studies demonstrated that these synthesized CDs serve as an efficient fluorescent imaging agent targeting cancer cells specifically [102] (Fig. 3 ). Hair, silk, and feathers are also considered an appealing candidates for preparing heteroatom-doped CDs. As poultry waste is a rich source of nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen and carbon, Ruili et al. utilized the goose feather as a precursor for synthesizing highly photoluminescent CDs by hydrothermal approach. The as-synthesized CDs showed 2D uniform morphology with a diameter of 21.5 nm and an efficiency of 17.1%. It highly functions as a nanosensor towards Fe3+ ions with a low detection limit of 196 nM [103]. CDs obtained from silkworm chrysalis with excellent solubility and stability in an aqueous medium exhibit excitation-dependent PL. Under an inverted fluorescence microscope, CDs display blue green and red colors, which showed their vast potential in bioimaging [104]. Commercial bee pollen, an economical and eco-friendly approach in the reported study, is chosen as a carbon source for synthesizing ultra-small size PL CDs. The high reproducibility of CDs increases the chances of their production on a large scale without affecting the mass conversion ratio. It showed promising application in bioimaging and light-emitting devices. The synthesized CDs at concentration 0.5 mg mL −1 are considered safe for cytotoxicity measurements as reported[105].

The carbon NPs of diameter 2 nm were developed from baked lamb and emitted strong blue fluorescence on exposure with UV light as reported by Wang et al [106]. In addition, they could serve as a fluorescence sensor for glucose detection through an oxidation–reduction reaction. Similarly, several more by-products of animals such as pig skin[107], crab shell [108], prawn shell[109], pigeon feathers [49], shrimp shells[110], duck blood [111], duck breast[112], whey[113], and spoiled milk [114] are also exploited for producing green CDs (Table 2 ).

Eggs are the primary requirement in our day-to-day lives, with abundant proteins, carbohydrates, and fat. The inconsiderable amount of eggshells is utilized in fertilizers but maximum is rejected as waste. Nowadays, it was utilized in the production of CDs via a simple and green strategy. One of the studies reported the fabrication of CDs from egg white. The as-produced CDs showed attractive fluorescent properties and excellent pH stability demonstrating its immense potential in biomedical applications and nanosensors [115]. Similarly, Wang et al. [116] reported the synthesis of luminescent N-CDs using boiled egg yolk. PL spectra observed the red shift of peaks with shifting in excitation wavelength indicating their potential application in multi-color imaging. Moreover, the presence of oxygen and hydrophilic groups on N-CDs increases its aqueous stability without precipitation for several months, making them available for bioimaging and energy storage applications. Mirza Muhammad et al. generated bright CDs by using inexpensive egg whites through a one-step heating reaction. As a fluorescent labeling agent, it can quantitatively detect the presence of curcumin from the complex samples. In addition, its excellent water solubility and high QY made them available for biosensing and imaging applications [117]. Chicken eggshell membrane (ESM) was utilized by Srikrishna Pramanik et al. for the synthesis of a highly fluorescent nanoprobe for base-pair selective and sequence-specific DNA recognition with a narrow particle size distribution of 1–5 nm[118].The isothermal calorimetric studies specify thermodynamically favorable binding of CDs with DNA.

Nowadays, utilizing the by-products from food processing industries as a carbon source is gaining prominence. CDs derived from these wastes successfully applied for sensing metal ion and bioimaging applications because of their low toxicity and outstanding photostability. A poorly exploited grape by-product (wine lees) produced during the wine-making process is excellent for creating CDs. Microwave and ultrasonic methods are operated for the extraction of the phenolic compounds from the wine lees (WLs). The results demonstrated that the microwave method provides better extraction efficiency than the conventional one [119]. Similarly, Varisco et al. produced CDs from WLs by direct combustion method for ink-jettable material with excellent PL properties [120]. The conversion of winery residues to a valuable material is a big step towards a green and sustainable environment. Recently, the disposal of millions of tons of waste frying oil has been of significant concern. When drained, it polluted the water and disturbed the ecosystem, and becoming the primary cause of human health problems. Yaoping et al [121] demonstrated the utilization of oil waste as a carbon source in creating CDs by carbonization with sulfuric acid. The as-prepared CDs with high photostability and satisfactory QY is applied in biomedical applications. Similarly, tofu yellow serofluid was applied for synthesizing two sorts of fluorescent CDs (3.5 nm) that can emit blue and green lights on irradiation with UV light. Further, CDs were characterized by FTIR, HRTEM, and XPS analysis. XPS study explained that the fundamental difference between two CDs is the content of C–O and C = O bonds [122]. Many researchers reported that sugarcane residues (bagasse) are also considered an excellent carbon source for the fabrication of CDs. In one of the studies, Surendran et al [123] fabricated the fluorescent CDs from bagasse hydrothermally with a QY of 17.98% and the particle size between 0.75 and 2.75 nm. It displayed the blue emission. The as synthesized CDs have promising material for NLO devices bioimaging, and pharmaceutical applications. The biorefinery by-products derived CDs showed stable fluorescence behavior under changing temperature with potential applications in vivo cell imaging. Similarly, several more food processed by-products such as sugar cane bagasse [124], sugar cane bagasse pulp[125], and sugarcane molasses[47] are also exploited for producing green CDs (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs from other waste materials
ce:section.ce:para: The conversion of industrial waste into value-added products is a primary focus for environmental scientists worldwide due to their non-biodegradable nature that poses a severe environmental threat. Their use as a source in producing the value-added product (CDs) is an advantage for the ecosystem by reducing the burden of the waste product that pollutes the environment. A polymer polyethylene terephthalate(PET) used as a packaging material produced vast quantities of solid wastes, which are hazardous and non-degradable. Recently, scientists found an alternative route to process the PET wastes as a promising feedstock for synthesizing CNTs, graphene, and CDs[126,127]. Yaoping et al [128], in their study demonstrated the use of PET-based plastic bottles for the development of fluorescent CDs by air oxidation/ hydrothermal method without utilizing any toxic chemicals and harsh synthesis conditions. Even in complex environmental and biological conditions, it exhibited the sensing behavior towards Fe3+ions and pyrophosphate (PPi). Theelectron transferbetween Fe3+ and CDs accounted for the PL quenching and recovery processes during Fe3+ and PPi detections. The color-tunable CDs synthesized from a waste paper by solvothermal method with different solvents could be successfully employed to prepare anti-counterfeiting ink and fluorescent flexible film with high photo and chemical stability and excellent optical properties [129].Waste paper and urea hydrothermally derived N-CDs as a photodetector shown strong blue-green luminescence with excellent optoelectronic properties [130]. Foam waste generated from polyurethane in the environment is an alarming situation. Due to its high nitrogen content, it is used as a precursor for producing highlyfluorescentCDs via the pyrolysis method. Under UV light, CDs manifested a blue-green emission withQYdetermined to be 33% and selectively detected the Ag± ions from real water samples with a limit of detection of 2.8 μM [131]. Similarly, several more food processed by-products such as plastic bottles [132], waste paper[133], kitchen chimney oil[134], candle soot[135], polystyrene waste[136], plastic waste[137], and carbonaceous materials[138] are also explored for producing green CDs (Table 3 ).

From the above discussion, we can conclude that among many techniques for synthesizing CDs, the hydrothermal method is preferred due to its low cost and environmentally friendly nature. The QY is dependent on the careful selection of the precursor and surface passivating agents. Moreover, different synthesis method is responsible for the differences in their size, chemical structure, color, fluorescence, QY, and toxicity. Fabrication of uniform size green CDs are challenging, which limits their biological applications. Surface modification and heteroatom doping improve the performance of CDs with good dispersion and stability. In general, the presence of electron-donating groups on the surface (–OH, -OR, –NH2, -NR2, –CN) increases the fluorescence intensity, while electron-withdrawing groups (–COOH, –NO2, -C = O) quench the intensity. For specific applications, surface passivation and functionalization of green CDs are carried out via coordination, covalent bonding, and π-π interaction, etc.

To understand the intrinsic properties of CDs, it is necessary to find out the mechanism of fluorescence, which plays a significant role in the utilization of CDs in sensing and imaging applications. The most accepted mechanisms are molecular fluorescence, quantum confinement effect, and surface state emission discussed below.

Fluorescent small molecules (molecular fluorophores) or fluorescent impurities produced during the bottom-up approach emit the fluorescence. These fluorophores bonded to the surface of the CDs and affected their optical properties. The mechanism claims that the number of produced fluorophores and carbon nucleus within a specific temperature range is closely related to the fluorescence properties of CDs [139-141]. Color CDs prepared from tomato, lemon juice, and pumpkin showed the PL behavior is related to the MF mechanism [142,143].

QCE appears when the size of CDs is smaller than the Bohr radius of the exciton that leads to the increase of band gap and blue shift. Due to the confined size of the conjugatedπdomain of CDs, the non-local molecular orbit of CDs separated the VB and CB, and electrons in CB directly moved to the VB contain holes without the defect centers' involvement responsible for the band gap fluorescence. The sp2 and sp3 hybrid C atoms in CDs are responsible for the direct recombination of e- and h+. Due to the small particle size, the sp2 carbon cluster is easily dispersed into the sp3 C skeleton. Electrons in energy levels of sp2 hybridization (πandπ*) are affected by the energy states of sp3 (σandσ*), and e- pairs from sp2 undergo transitions and produce fluorescence [139]. The band gap of CDs can be tuned by adjusting the size of the conjugatedπdomain, which in turn modulates the color-emitting characteristics of CDs. Li et al. observed red shift emission in CDs with an increase in size (5 to 35 nm) caused by QCE[144].

Oxidation of surface and surface functional groups are responsible for the surface defect state fluorescence. Surface defects are divided into two types: purity and impurity defects. Topological defects and oxygen and nitrogen-containing defects (functional groups on the surface) are included in the purity defects. Fluorescence originating from these defects is characterized by pleochroism and excitation-dependent luminescence. On irradiation by the light of a specific wavelength, photons on CDs transit and accumulate in the traps of adjacent surface defects and emit visible light of different wavelengths when returning to the ground state [145]. The red-shifted emissions depend on the presence of oxygen content on CDs. Increasing oxygen content increases the surface defects and emission sites that led to the red-shifted emission [146,147]. In addition, the different functional groups have different energy levels in CDs which alter the fluorescence. The higher the ability of the functional group to generate electrons, the higher is the generated energy[148,149].

CDs have received immense attention and made them available for many biomedical and electrochemical applications because of their distinctive properties like lower toxicity, chemical inertness, good photostability, high water solubility, and high yield. Few of them are discussed.

CDs have been successfully employed in bioimaging due to their distinctive characteristic properties over conventional molecular-scale labels. Excellent optical and electrical properties have defined surface functionalization, multimeric binding protocols, and a large variety of multiplexing strategies. Prior to its application in bioimaging, toxicity should be tested. The toxicity of CDs depends on multiple factors that are directly correlated to environmental conditions and physicochemical properties. Moreover, oxidative, photolytic, and mechanical stability have also been concerned as determining factors in toxicity. The intrinsically non-toxic elemental composition of CDs allowed them to be used for bio-analytical applications[22]. For bioimaging applications, appropriate CD concentration is determined by cell viability experiments.

CDs (100 μg/mL)synthesized from sweet potato successfully improved the cell viability of human epithelial carcinoma (HeLa) and human hepatocarcinoma (HepG2)cells and emitted blue, green, and red (BGR) light upon excitation with different λex on the penetration of CD inside the cells [150]. Similarly, papaya peel-derived CDs used to treat HeLa cells (CD concentration: 50 mg L-1, cell viability > 95%). Cells emitted blue and green light upon excitation at a wavelength of 420 and 520 nm [55]. Tulsi leaves, and coriander leaves were utilized to treat breast and lung cancer cells [151,152]. CDs hydrothermally fabricated fromM. liliifloraflower injected in the Clone 9 hepatocytes and emitted the BGR (blue-green–red) fluorescence at different λex as shown in Fig. 4
[153]. CDs produced from dried shrimp utilized as drug carriers of boldine, when injected into the nucleus of MCF-7 cells(breast cancer cell line) showed induced death of the cells compared with free boldine and normal cells [154]. Lychee exocarp-derived CDs is successfully destroyed the tumor cell in mice in 21 days, as reported[155].

Green CDs are also used as an effective drug delivery carrier because of their small size and high biocompatibility that enables them to be easily absorbed by the cells. Sarkar et al.[156] prepared CDs from the aloe vera leaf gel for the delivery of Vancomycin (antibiotic drug) in the gastrointestinal tract by coating over the film of calcium alginate (CA), which later on combined with β-cyclodextrin to form a hybrid. The resulting combination showed excellent loading efficiency of 96% and sustained drug release at pH 1.5 for a period of 120 h. Fluorescent CDs generated from carrot roots showed biocompatibility towards MCF-7 cells. These CDs also showed pH-responsive drug release behavior towards the Mitomycin (anticancer drug)[157]. On conjugation with milk-derived CDs, doxorubicin targeted the Adenoid cancer cells (ACC-2) and internalized the drug into the tumor cells [158]. Similarly, CDs produced from gum Arabic deliver the antibiotic drug ciprofloxacin in bacterial cells[159].Delivering doxorubicin to cervical cancer cells (HeLa) was supported by CDs prepared from black tea [160].

CDs are considered the most favorable candidate for detecting metal ions and molecules due to their low cytotoxicity, small size, and improved chemical and photostability. The change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs on the interaction of their functional groups with the external chemical species by creating a new electron-hole pair is considered a measurable response signal for chemical sensing applications.

The sensing mechanism of CDs based on fluorescence quenching is dependent on the following pathways [161] as shown in Fig. 5 . (1) Electron transfer mechanism (ET): Under UV light irradiation, electrons of CDs moved to the CB of the metal ions via ET pathway (2) Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET): From the excited state of CDs, energy is transferred to the metal ions. (3) Inner filler effect (IFE): Re-absorption of energy by metal ions emitted from CDs. (4) Donation of electrons by CDs to the metal ion (d-orbital). (5) Aggregate induced emission quenching (AIEQ): Without radiative emission, energy from the excited state of CDs is transferred to the ground state during CDs aggregation. (6) Static quenching effect (SQE): Upon photoexcitation, in the ground state, complex is formed in between metal ion quenchers and CDs. All these pathways are included in dynamic quenching except SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions using green CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pomelo peel was utilized by Lu and its group for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions[162]. Interaction of Hg2+ with the carboxyl group of CDs leads to the fluorescent quenching (ET/FRET) by generating e- -h± pair. Fluorescence intensity increased with increasing pH (1–7) and decreased with further increase in pH (8–13). Similarly, corn bract derived CDs were employed for the detection of Hg2+ ions from the real water samples. The nanohybrid showed two emission peaks (470 and 678 nm) with single excitation. Chlorophyll-derived porphyrins emission peak at 678 nm quenched on addition of metal ion, due to the interaction between ions and aromatic ring [86].Water-soluble CDs derived from lemon peel were used for the selective detection of Cr6+ ions in the linear range of 2.5–50 μM (detection limit: 73 nM) [163]. Likewise, groundnut detect the Cr6+ ions via ET/FRET mechanism[27]. Spoiled milk-derived CDs as reported[114] could recognize Cr6+ ions (detection limit: 14 µM). Liao et al. demonstrated the applicability of CDs derived from peach gum to detect Au3+ ions from river water samples via FRET transfer from CDs to the metal ions[164]. CDs prepared from the chocolate and Osmium sanctum selectively detect Pb2+ ions via ET mechanism (detection limit: 12.7 nM, and 0.67 nM) [165,166]. Mushroom-based CDs successfully sense the Pb2+ ions with limit of detection (LoD) and limit of quantitation (LoQ) determined to be 58.63 and 177.69 µM [167]. The sensing of Fe3+ ions using green CDs is reported in many studies. Plant leaves[168], honey[38], and banana stem[169] are used to sense Fe3+ ions. The sensing mechanism depends on the interaction between the functional groups on CDs and metal ions that lead to the quenching via SQE/ET/FRET mechanism. It may be the result of electron transfer from CDs to the vacant orbital of Fe3+ ions. Similarly, Ginkgo biloba leaves, Tulsi leaves, cranberry, and blueberry, selectively detect the Fe3+ ions (concentration range: 0.1–20.0 nM, 30–600 and 12.5–100 µM) respectively. Hydrothermally synthesized CDs from mint leaves [170], banana[171], tea leaves[99], coriander leaves [153], and M. liliiflora [154] are also successfully sensed the Fe3+ ion. The LODs determined to be 0.37, 0.21, 0.25, 0.40 and 1.2 µM in Fe3+ ion concentration ranges of 0–0.38, 0–50, 0.25–60, 0–60 and 1–1000 µM respectively. N-doped CDs synthesized from grass having nitrogen and oxygen atoms on their surface chelate successfully with the Cu2+ ions with a detection limit of 1 μM [172]. Similarly, eggshell membrane[173], bamboo leaves[174], and pipe tobacco[175] have also been used as fluorescent sensors to detect Cu2+ions via ET/IFE/FRET mechanism.

Green CDs also sense bioactive molecules and mechanism is similar to that of chemical sensing. Wang et al. [173] utilized eggshell membrane-derived CDs to sense the glutathione via microwave combustion. With the addition of Cu2+ ions, PL intensity was suppressed via ET mechanism, further addition of GSH in the system completely removed the Cu2+ions from the surface of CDs by developing the coordination with metal ion and resulted in the recovery of fluorescence. For glucose sensing in human blood, willow bark- derived CDs were utilized. Oxidation product of glucose (H2O2) electrochemically detected by cyclic voltammetry (CV)[176]. The fluorescence of konjac flour (heteropolysaccharides) derived N-CDs was quenched on the addition of Fe3+ ion (ET mechanism) and was recovered on the addition of basic amino acids by increasing the pH of the solution[177]. Salep flour-derived CDs, in combination with silver, formed a nanocomposite, successfully detecting H2O2 using CV. The detection limit was determined to be 80 nM (linear range: 0.2–27.0 μM)[178]. Similarly, CDs produced from cornstalks also detect the H2O2 in the presence of Fe2+ ions with detection limit found to be 0.21 μM[179].

Compared to other common photocatalysts (ZnO, CdS, TiO2), green CDs worked as an efficient photocatalyst due to their light absorption ability in the NIR region, non toxic nature, and excellent water solubility[180,181]. Under UV irradiation, peels of waste orange derived CDs on conjugation with ZnO were used to degrade napthol blue black azo dye. Electrons are moved from the VB of ZnO to the CB and later transferred to the CB of CDs, leaving behind the e--h± pair on the surface of ZnO. The latter then produced superoxide and hydroxyl radicals on interaction with oxygen and water that promote the degradation of the dye. Willow bark[176] and bee pollen[182]-derived -derived CDs showed excellent photocatalytic activity to reduce gold, silver, and chloroaurate ions under UV and solar light irradiation. CDs produced from lemon peels conjugated with TiO2 and form TiO2-CD nanocomposite. It worked as an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue, as reported by Tyagi et al.[58]. Green CDs also worked as an efficient electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction process. The as-synthesized CDs demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic activity even after 20,000 cycles without a decrease in the catalytic current compared to the commercial Pt electrode[183].

The inks that are not visible during daytime become detectable under UV light at a specific wavelength are considered fluorescent inks. CDs, due to their unique characteristic properties such as their superior fluorescent nature, non-toxic behavior, and PL stability, etc., have the potential to be utilized in fluorescent ink applications. Fluorescent inks prepared from CDs are eco-friendly, permanent, and transparent, and favorably employed in information encryption, printing, and anti-counterfeiting. Prunus cerasifera generated CDs used as fluorescent ink for writing on filter paper (365 nm). The ink emitted blue light that helps visualize the writing on the paper [184]. Similarly, CDs fabricated from natural fruit juice [185], Piper betel[186], and banana peel [187] are exploited to prepare fluorescent ink for printing snowflake patterns and graphic patterns on the paper with bright blue fluorescence. The aqueous CDs derived from the betel leaves are considered a promising candidate for fluorescent ink application. Hand-written letters and drawings were invisible under daylight, but when irradiated with a UV lamp, it is visible and emitted bright blue fluorescence. After storing for 20 weeks, no noticeable change was observed in images/letters, which suggested the excellent fluorescence stability of CDs, as shown in Fig. 6. .

This review summarizes the recent advancement in production, properties, and applications of CDs derived from natural and renewable resources. Major applications like bioimaging, drug delivery, chemical sensing, catalysis, and fluorescent inks are discussed briefly. The presence of various functional groups on green CDs eliminates the need for surface-passivating agents. Different natural food and its wastes are also used for the preparation of CDs. Utilizing green resources as a precursor is beneficial in producing CDs and aids in the evolution of sustainable management. Even though many methods are reported for the preparation of CDs, we focused on a most recurrent method that is simple and environmental-friendly, i.e., hydrothermal method. CDs' morphology, composition, and structure are characterized by HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, Raman, and XPS studies. Green CDs successfully utilized for detecting metal ions and small molecules based on the quenching mechanism (ET, FRET, IFE, and AIEQ), show high sensitivity and selectivity. CDs have become a fascinating material for the bioimaging of mammalian cells and bacteria. In addition, the catalytic efficiency of CDs is enhanced by conjugating it with a photocatalyst (semiconductor) by the electron transfer mechanism and reduced charge recombination. Biomass-derived CDs have also been explored for clinical medication by studying the metabolic process of the drug in animals on combining with drug molecules. Although significant improvement has been achieved in the past decade, some challenges need to be resolved.

1. Despite extensive research on the synthesis and modification of CDs, the actual process of CDs production remains unknown. The fluorescence quantum yields in the majority of CDs were determined to be low, limiting its application. The main goal is to optimize the synthesis methods further to improve the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs.

2. Commercial-scale fabrication of exceptional class CDs from biomass waste is still a big challenge. Maximum of the research has been conducted on a laboratory scale. The commercial-scale production using green precursors to produce CDs is not imminent so far; that’s why it is necessary to be known whether the characteristic properties of samples generated from a large scale exhibited the same properties as those produced on the lab scale. Extensive studies should be carried out towards the production of CDs on a large scale from green precursors.
3. Mechanism of photoluminescent is still uncertain. High need to investigate the in-depth PL mechanism of CDs generated from renewable resources to expand its application.

4. Synthesis of long-wavelength emissive CDs is challenging. As reported, most of the synthesized CDs have maximal absorption wavelength in the region of ultraviolet/visible, limiting its biomedical and photocatalytic applications. NIR light is used explicitly for biomedical applications due to its low biological toxicity and deep tissue penetration. More efforts should be needed in designing the strategy for the synthesis of CDs with NIR absorbance and emission.

5. During the synthesis, precisely controlling the surface functionalization of CDs is an upsetting fact. The surface of biomass-derived CDs has many functional groups responsible for its good solubility, but on the other hand, it suppresses the fluorescence of CDs. As a fluorescent probe, CDs worked on the concept of the fluorescence-quenching mechanism. However, it is unclear whether the analytes of interest or any other factor is accountable for quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Excellent selectivity of CDs is highly desirable for any application.

6. Large quantity of residues is produced during the “Top-down” synthesis of CDs, amounts exceeding those of the CDs. Dealing with reducing the by-products is still a challenging factor. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the methods that maximize the conversion of raw materials into CDs without any loss of nanomaterials.

7. Despite the applicability of green CDs as a chemical sensor in real samples, studies on metal ions (Zn2+, As3+, Ag+, Cd2+, Po3+, Mn2+) are still missing from the literature. To elucidate the full potential of these CDs, research on these metal ions should be undertaken. Moreover, increasing the sensitivity and selectivity is still a challenge that can be improved by the right selection of precursors, which defines the chemical nature of CDs. In addition, the development of ratiometric and bi-modular detection is also helpful in achieving high sensitivity and good selectivity.

8. Compared to the chemically derived CDs, utilization of green CDs as photocatalyst is limited. Therefore, research should be extended in the area of photocatalysis by using green CDs. The careful selection of precursors is significant to improve photocatalytic activities. In addition, most of the studies reported the CDs as photocatalyst emit green and blue light, which indicates its limited light collection capacity. Therefore, CDs with longer emission wavelength should be developed to extend the process in the visible region. Besides, NMR and time-resolved spectroscopies should be investigated to understand the mechanism of degradation catalyzed by CDs.
8. Increased attention is required on utilizing CDs on photodynamic and photothermal therapy.

9. CDs as an adsorbent opened a new gateway for developing highly and inexpensive, efficient water treatment techniques. However, some bottlenecks should be overcome (a) uncertainty in the relationship between the precursor and adsorption capacity. (b) Difficulty in separation for recycling purposes. Therefore, further research on conditions, method, raw material and mechanism of adsorption should be extensively required for CD-based adsorbents with high adsorption capacity. To understand the adsorbent's in-depth mechanism, theoretical simulation and prediction should be performed, promoting the future development of CDs-based adsorbents for environmental applications.

10. Size distribution of CDs plays a vital role in determining their fluorescence behavior and nature. Inadequate size distribution of CDs hampers its bio-applications (vivo). Moreover, insufficient reproducibility of CDs concerning their size, intensity, QY, etc., also limits their production on a large scale. Therefore, new strategies should be introduced that are efficient, economically sustainable, and facile for bulk production.

There are still many challenges in the way to utilize the tremendous potential of green CDs despite being many advances. We hope that this review gives a thought to the upcoming exciting applications by assembling the existing information on CDs. Undoubtedly CDs will improve significant interest in the future.
